text:
MULTIPHASIC CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST AND CT CHEST
PANCREATIC PROTOCOL — RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the head and uncinate process. She was initially staged as borderline resectable (T4 N1 M0) with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and direct SMV contact with partial luminal compromise. She received neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles (with Eloxatin dose reduction for neuropathy) followed by consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent cape, which was completed approximately 2 months prior to this examination. This restaging study is obtained to evaluate tumor response in anticipation of multidisciplinary surgical reassessment. Prior examinations available for comparison: CT abdomen/pelvis at mid-treatment following 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX, and CT abdomen/pelvis/chest following completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT abdomen and pelvis performed using dedicated pancreatic protocol: unenhanced, arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), portal venous, and delayed phase acquisitions following intravenous administration of 100 mL iohexol (Omnipaque 350) at a flow rate of 4 mL/sec, with multiplanar reformations in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Field of view: diaphragm to symphysis pubis. CT chest performed as a separate acquisition: 3 mm axial sections through the thorax without intravenous contrast, with lung window and mediastinal window reconstructions. Oral contrast administered. Note: Examination is interpreted in the context of prior biliary stent placement; no biliary stent is identified on current examination.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear. No focal parenchymal opacity, consolidation, or ground-glass infiltrates. No pulmonary nodules are identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesion.

Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thyroid gland is within normal limits in the visualized portion. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Cardiovascular: Heart size is normal. Aorta demonstrates mild atherosclerotic calcification without aneurysmal dilation. Coronary artery calcifications noted, consistent with the patient's known cardiovascular risk factors. No pericardial effusion.

Bone Windows (Thorax): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions in the thoracic spine, ribs, or sternum to suggest metastatic involvement.

---

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Pancreas — Primary Tumor:
In the head and uncinate process of the pancreas, there persists a heterogeneously hypoenhancing soft tissue mass with irregular margins and associated dense desmoplastic reaction. On current examination, the mass measures approximately 2.3 x 1.9 cm in the axial plane (previously 2.6 x 2.1 cm at the post-8-cycle mFOLFIRINOX scan, 3.1 x 2.5 cm at mid-treatment, and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at baseline). This represents continued, progressive dimensional reduction from prior examinations, consistent with an ongoing treatment response. The lesion demonstrates only modest enhancement relative to surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on pancreatic parenchymal phase images, with a persistent hypodense appearance. The surrounding desmoplastic halo remains present but appears less prominent and less extensive than on prior studies. There is no new internal necrosis or gas to suggest abscess formation.

Vascular Relationships — Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA):
At baseline, the primary tumor contacted the SMA over more than 180 degrees of circumference, the defining criterion for borderline/locally advanced disease. On the post-4-cycle scan, this contact had reduced to approximately 90-120 degrees. On the post-8-cycle scan, this was estimated at approximately 90 degrees or less. On the current examination, the soft tissue at the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA now contacts the artery over an estimated arc of approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. The SMA wall itself appears smooth and without encasement. The fat plane between the residual tumor mass and the right lateral SMA wall is extremely narrow but is appreciable on thin-section reconstructions, particularly on delayed phase imaging. There is no gross SMA infiltration or irregular narrowing of the arterial lumen to suggest intimal involvement.

Vascular Relationships — Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Confluence:
Previous examinations demonstrated direct tumor contact with the SMV with mild luminal narrowing. Following the completion of chemoradiation, the SMV now demonstrates a patent lumen with maintained caliber throughout its course. There is minimal residual soft tissue thickening along the right lateral wall of the SMV at the prior site of most intimate contact; however, this does not result in any appreciable luminal compromise. The portal confluence is patent. The superior mesenteric vein flows smoothly into the portal vein without web, thrombus, or occlusion. No cavernous transformation is noted. The celiac axis and common hepatic artery are patent and unremarkable.

Main Pancreatic Duct:
The main pancreatic duct diameter has normalized compared to baseline (where it measured 6 mm). Current diameter is approximately 3 mm in the body of the pancreas upstream of the mass, which represents near-complete normalization of the previously noted obstructive dilation. This is likely a reflection of the diminished obstructive burden of the residual mass.

Biliary System:
The intrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct is not discretely identified distal to the pancreatic head given the known tumor, consistent with prior course. No biliary stent is present. No pneumobilia. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without gallstones or wall thickening.

Regional and Distant Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, periportal, celiac, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric regions. The previously noted peripancreatic lymph node of concern (1.2 cm at baseline, 0.7 cm at mid-treatment, approximately 0.5 cm at post-8-cycle scan) is no longer clearly seen as a discrete nodal structure; there is a very small soft tissue density in this region measuring approximately 4 mm, indeterminate in significance but not appreciably enlarged. No para-aortic or iliac nodal enlargement.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Liver parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement throughout all phases. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypodense, hypervascular, or rim-enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic deposits. Previously noted absence of hepatic metastases is maintained. No biliary ductal dilation within the liver. Hepatic veins and portal venous system are patent.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneously enhancing. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal mass or nodule identified.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys demonstrate normal size, shape, and cortical thickness with prompt, symmetric contrast enhancement. No solid renal masses. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding. A 5 mm simple cortical cyst is noted in the left kidney, unchanged from prior examination.

Stomach and Bowel:
The stomach is unremarkable. Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness. The large bowel is normal in caliber. No obstruction. No wall thickening or mucosal irregularity to suggest radiation enteritis or colitis. No pneumatosis. No free air.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
The peritoneal surfaces are smooth. There is no peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or serosal implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The mesentery is homogeneous without soft tissue stranding beyond the expected post-treatment changes in the peripancreatic region. No ascites.

Abdominal Aorta and Vasculature:
The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification. No aneurysmal dilation. Celiac axis, splenic artery, common hepatic artery, and SMA origins appear patent. The inferior vena cava is patent throughout.

Bone Windows (Abdomen and Pelvis):
No lytic, sclerotic, or mixed osseous lesions in the thoracic or lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones to suggest metastatic disease. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine at L3-4 and L4-5 are noted, unchanged.

Pelvis:
The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable given patient's age. Urinary bladder is normally distended and smooth-walled. No pelvic free fluid. No iliac or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. CONTINUED DIMENSIONAL REDUCTION OF PANCREATIC HEAD/UNCINATE MASS following completion of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) and consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy with concurrent cape). The lesion now measures approximately 2.3 x 1.9 cm, compared to 2.6 x 2.1 cm on the most recent prior scan and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at initial presentation. The cumulative reduction from baseline is substantial, consistent with meaningful locoregional treatment effect.

2. MARKEDLY IMPROVED VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS. Contact of the residual mass with the superior mesenteric artery has diminished considerably across serial examinations and is currently estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of arc. The previously noted SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees has undergone sustained, progressive reduction. An extremely narrow fat plane is now perceptible adjacent to the right lateral SMA wall on thin reconstructions. The SMV is fully patent without luminal compromise; only minimal residual soft tissue thickening persists at the prior SMV contact site. Overall vascular anatomy now appears substantially more favorable than at initial staging.

3. NO EVIDENCE OF DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE. No hepatic lesions, no pulmonary nodules, no peritoneal implants, no osseous lesions, and no pathologically enlarged lymphadenopathy identified.

4. NEAR-COMPLETE RESOLUTION OF PANCREATIC DUCT DILATION. Main pancreatic duct now measures approximately 3 mm, essentially normalized compared to baseline (6 mm), indicating reduced obstructive effect from the treated primary lesion.

5. The overall radiographic picture is one of a meaningful and ongoing treatment response. The residual soft tissue density at the primary tumor site may represent viable residual tumor, treatment-related fibrosis, or a combination of both — distinction cannot be made on imaging alone and will require histopathologic assessment at the time of surgical resection.

These findings support multidisciplinary surgical reassessment for potential pancreaticoduodenectomy. The vascular anatomy as currently depicted may permit resection with intent to achieve negative margins, though final determination of technical resectability remains the domain of the surgical team with intraoperative evaluation.

Correlation with serum CA 19-9 and multidisciplinary tumor board review is advised.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division]
Clinical Service: Pancreatic Oncology / Hepatopancreatobiliary Surgery
